title: Rubber Duck
purpose: Render a stylised rubber duck: a fat yellow body, yellow head merging into the body, an up-swept tail, an orange beak protruding forward, two black eyes, and a dark matte floor for grounding, under soft studio lighting.
preview_note: octane-preview.png is the native Octane X render from 2026-07-15 after the material-binding fix (the assign handler now passes material_name as the pin-match hint; previously all parts rendered white). Pixel QA: 1280x1280, 272,683 bytes, foreground 54.9%, contrast 91.3, likely_blank=false. Central-region sample: ~5.8% clearly-yellow body pixels + 27.6% warm midtones. Bridge log shows 'material mat_body -> pin mat_body' etc. for all four materials.
quality_checklist: The duck reads as a duck at thumbnail size: rounded body, head, beak, eyes, tail.
The body and head are yellow (glossy), the beak is orange, the eyes are black.
Explicit create_material + assign_material commands bind every OBJ usemtl group by MATERIAL NAME (not group_index).
A dark matte floor grounds the shot and gives the duck a contact shadow.
Camera is a front-three-quarter hero with the duck filling roughly one-third to one-half the frame.
known_pitfalls: The assign_material bridge handler MUST pass material_name as the pin-match hint; without it the mesh pins (named after the OBJ usemtl group) never match and every part renders white.
OBJ/MTL colours are only hints; Octane colour correctness depends on the explicit create_material + assign_material commands in scene.json.
Per-vertex colours baked into the OBJ are NOT honoured by the Octane X OBJ importer on this build (usedColor stays white), so named-material binding is the only reliable colour path.
